title: Light the Candle When You Walk In
subtitle: A daily arrival ritual for marking the transition from the outside world to home
excerpt: A simple daily ritual for arriving home: light a candle as the first act of being there. The small ceremony of arrival. For evenings when you walk in and the day follows you straight through the door.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on threshold rituals found across many cultures: acts that mark the boundary between outside and home.

practice.whenToUse: Use as a daily arrival ritual on evenings when the stress of the day follows you home, or when the transition between outside world and home life needs a clearer marker.

content.text: Many cultures have always used a threshold act to mark the passage between outside and home. The act is not about the candle. It is about the pause: the conscious recognition that you have crossed a boundary and that a different mode of being is available now.

Place a candle somewhere you pass on the way in: the hallway, the kitchen, the table near the door. Put a lighter or matches next to it. The ritual only works if it requires no decision-making. The location, the candle, and the lighter are all ready before you need them.
When you walk in, before you check your phone, before you start talking, before you begin anything: light the candle. One act. It takes three seconds.
Watch the flame for one breath. Just one. Long enough to register that you have arrived. The day is behind you. The home is in front of you.
Say it once if you want to. Or just let the pause be enough.
Original to homemade.education. Threshold rituals, acts that mark the passage between outside world and home, appear across many cultures.
